claim: State-of-the-art LLM serving performance on Nvidia GPUs requires matching 800,000+ tokens per second on benchmarks like the Llama 3 model to be considered credible.
actionable: Use high-throughput token-per-second benchmarks (800k+ tps on Llama 3) as your minimum acceptance threshold when evaluating inference systems.
source_title: The Shape of Compute (Chris Lattner of Modular)

claim: A clean, modular compiler architecture with distinct front-end, optimizer, and code-generator layers enables faster hardware support and evolution compared to monolithic designs like GCC.
actionable: Design your AI/ML infrastructure with clean separation of concerns across parsing, optimization, and code generation phases to reduce the cost of supporting new hardware.
source_title: The Shape of Compute (Chris Lattner of Modular)

claim: GPU kernel optimization via automatic compiler-level fusion (e.g., fusing attention with activation functions) reduces the need to handwrite all kernel permutations and improves performance.
actionable: Use compiler-driven kernel fusion to automatically combine small GPU operations instead of hand-writing all combinations; check frameworks like Modular MAX for this capability.
source_title: The Shape of Compute (Chris Lattner of Modular)

claim: Custom GPU programming at the PTX level (tensor core instruction set) is standard practice for performance-critical AI research teams, not unique to specific companies.
actionable: Learn and optimize at the PTX instruction set level for performance-critical components rather than relying solely on high-level frameworks when maximum speed is needed.
source_title: The Shape of Compute (Chris Lattner of Modular)

claim: New GPU architectures (e.g., Blackwell) break kernel compatibility with prior generations (Hopper), requiring complete rewrites of optimized inference code.
actionable: Plan for hardware-specific kernel rewrites every 12-18 months as new GPU architectures are released; design abstractions to minimize rewrite scope.
source_title: The Shape of Compute (Chris Lattner of Modular)

claim: Prefix caching and page-attention techniques are critical standard optimizations in modern LLM serving frameworks.
actionable: Ensure your inference stack implements prefix caching and page-attention mechanisms to achieve competitive serving performance and throughput.
source_title: The Shape of Compute (Chris Lattner of Modular)

claim: Mojo as a Python-like compiled language enables 10,000x+ speedups over pure Python for performance-critical code while maintaining language coherence (no C/Rust bindings layer).
actionable: Use Mojo to replace Python bottlenecks in data processing pipelines and continuous batching logic where C++ or Rust binding complexity is not acceptable.
source_title: The Shape of Compute (Chris Lattner of Modular)

claim: An emerging pattern in AI inference is that both stateless CPU serving (from traditional cloud) and stateful GPU serving (for LLMs) require fundamentally different architectural approaches; GPU workloads do not horizontally autoscale.
actionable: For GPU-based workloads, abandon horizontal autoscaling assumptions and adopt cluster-level routing and capacity planning instead; stateless CPUs and stateful GPUs are incompatible deployment models.
source_title: The Shape of Compute (Chris Lattner of Modular)

claim: R&D projects focused on 'impossible' problems (like building a CUDA alternative) require deliberate secrecy and small teams to avoid external pressure and maintain focus on core technical milestones.
actionable: When pursuing technically ambitious R&D goals, keep internal team tight and public communication minimal until achieving a narrow, reproducible technical proof-of-concept.
source_title: The Shape of Compute (Chris Lattner of Modular)

claim: Once an infrastructure project achieves end-to-end functionality, switching from R&D to engineering mode (refactoring, adding features, deprecating APIs) dramatically accelerates velocity and product maturity.
actionable: After achieving a working prototype, immediately shift from exploratory R&D to disciplined feature delivery with 6-week release cadences and systematic engineering practices.
source_title: The Shape of Compute (Chris Lattner of Modular)

claim: AI coding tools (e.g., Cursor) enable zero-to-hero learning on new languages and frameworks, especially when combined with well-indexed, open-source codebases.
actionable: To onboard engineers onto a new language or framework, pair open-source implementations with code-indexing AI tools (Cursor, Claude) for rapid skill acquisition.
source_title: The Shape of Compute (Chris Lattner of Modular)

claim: Code as human communication matters more than code generation: long-term maintainability requires readable, understandable code even in an AI-assisted world.
actionable: Prioritize code readability and expressiveness in your AI/ML infrastructure; use languages and abstractions that humans can reason about constraints (latency, memory, cost).
source_title: The Shape of Compute (Chris Lattner of Modular)

claim: Launching a new programming language as 1.0 (as with Swift at Apple) creates community friction and perception of instability; better to launch as 0.x and gradually stabilize.
actionable: Release early-stage languages and frameworks with honest versioning (0.x) to manage expectations and feedback rather than declaring 1.0 prematurely.
source_title: The Shape of Compute (Chris Lattner of Modular)

claim: Using AI coding tools for boilerplate and mechanical coding works well; they are less effective for inventing novel algorithms or domain-specific optimizations.
actionable: Leverage AI coding assistants for routine scaffolding, test setup, and boilerplate; reserve human effort for algorithmic innovation and complex optimization decisions.
source_title: The Shape of Compute (Chris Lattner of Modular)

claim: Inference scales with the size of your customer base; training scales with research team size—this gap is where production-critical work lives.
actionable: Invest in inference optimization and deployment infrastructure once your model is production-ready; it impacts every user, unlike training which only affects your next research iteration.
source_title: The Shape of Compute (Chris Lattner of Modular)

claim: Continuous batching and KV cache eviction policies (including hashing algorithms) run primarily on CPU and are critical for keeping GPUs fully utilized in inference.
actionable: Optimize CPU-side batch scheduling and memory management alongside GPU kernels; CPU latency directly impacts GPU utilization in stateful LLM serving.
source_title: The Shape of Compute (Chris Lattner of Modular)
